primary_diagnosis.name: Decompensated alcohol-related liver disease (acute-on-chronic) with hepatic encephalopathy and ascites

comorbidities.name: Risk of thiamine deficiency / Wernicke's encephalopathy

pathophysiology_summary: Years of sustained heavy alcohol intake have produced chronic alcohol-related liver injury (steatohepatitis progressing to cirrhosis), evidenced by the stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider angiomas, palmar erythema) and by portal hypertension causing ascites. The patient has now acutely decompensated: failing hepatic clearance of nitrogenous waste raises circulating ammonia and other neurotoxins, producing hepatic encephalopathy (confusion, drowsiness, disorientation to time, and asterixis — the flapping tremor). Hyperbilirubinaemia produces jaundice and scleral icterus; portal hypertension and hypoalbuminaemia drive ascites. This state carries a high near-term risk of three life-threatening complications the clinician was trying to exclude: spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (infection of ascitic fluid), variceal haemorrhage (portal-hypertensive gastro-oesophageal varices), and progression of encephalopathy to hepatic coma. Poor adherence to thiamine adds a superimposed risk of Wernicke's encephalopathy, so parenteral thiamine must precede any glucose. Sedatives and benzodiazepines can precipitate or deepen hepatic encephalopathy and must be avoided outside supervised withdrawal management.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated alcohol-related liver disease with hepatic encephalopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Jaundice, abdominal distension and new confusion in a chronic heavy drinker is the classic decompensation triad.
Confusion in a drinker can be attributed to intoxication — an anchoring trap; does not explain jaundice or ascites.
Can cause jaundice, but does not explain ascites, asterixis or the encephalopathy pattern.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the danger is under-recognising decompensated liver disease and anchoring on 'just drunk'.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated alcohol-related liver disease with hepatic encephalopathy and ascites
differential.evidence_basis: Week of progressive jaundice + tense ascites + 48h of new confusion + asterixis + chronic heavy alcohol use.
Chronic alcohol use with poor thiamine adherence — must be covered by empirical parenteral thiamine; classic ophthalmoplegia/ataxia not clearly present but risk is high.
Cannot account for jaundice, ascites or asterixis.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated alcohol-related liver disease with hepatic encephalopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed decompensation pattern; the encephalopathy trigger (infection, GI bleed, constipation, electrolyte derangement) is unknown and must be sought in hospital.
Ascites plus encephalopathy mandates excluding SBP by diagnostic ascitic tap — impossible via telehealth; no fever now does not exclude it.
Portal hypertension carries high variceal-bleed risk; currently no haematemesis/melaena reported, but it is the most time-critical escalation edge to safety-net.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated alcohol-related liver disease with hepatic encephalopathy and ascites — requires emergency assessment to exclude SBP and variceal bleed
differential.evidence_basis: Telehealth can recognise the decompensation pattern and its red flags but cannot confirm the diagnosis or exclude the life-threatening complications; emergency transfer is mandated.

red_flags.description: New-onset confusion, drowsiness and disorientation (hepatic encephalopathy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Altered mental status in decompensated liver disease can progress to hepatic coma; it is a marker of hepatic failure and mandates emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to obtundation, airway compromise and hepatic coma if not escalated.

red_flags.description: Asterixis (coarse flapping tremor of outstretched hands)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A hallmark neurological sign of hepatic encephalopathy, corroborating hepatic decompensation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise encephalopathy and under-triage of a confused patient.

red_flags.description: Jaundice with new tense ascites (hepatic decompensation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals failing synthetic and excretory liver function with portal hypertension; high short-term mortality if untreated.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed decompensation and its treatable precipitants (infection, bleed).

red_flags.description: Risk of variceal / upper GI haemorrhage — vomiting blood or coffee-ground vomit, or black tarry stools
red_flags.clinical_significance: Portal hypertension makes catastrophic variceal bleeding a constant threat; it is the single most time-critical complication to safety-net for.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed variceal bleed can cause rapid exsanguination and death.

red_flags.description: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis — fever, rigors or worsening abdominal pain on a background of ascites
red_flags.clinical_significance: SBP is a common precipitant of encephalopathy and carries high mortality; it cannot be excluded without an ascitic tap, so absence of fever now does not clear it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated SBP leads to sepsis, hepatorenal syndrome and death.

red_flags.description: Marked ataxia / unsteadiness with high fall risk in a cluttered home
red_flags.clinical_significance: A confused, unsteady patient is at immediate risk of injurious falls and cannot safely self-transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fall with head injury or fracture while awaiting care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the confusion to 'he's just been drinking' and under-triaging — the jaundice, ascites and asterixis mark hepatic encephalopathy, not simple intoxication.
Agreeing to let the patient wait until morning or sleep it off — new encephalopathy with decompensation is an emergency and delay risks coma or a fatal bleed.
Recognising decompensation but failing to safety-net for SBP and variceal haemorrhage — the two treatable, time-critical complications that telehealth cannot exclude.
Reaching for a sedative or benzodiazepine to settle the confused/agitated patient — this precipitates and deepens hepatic encephalopathy and masks deterioration.
Giving glucose without prior parenteral thiamine in a chronic drinker can precipitate Wernicke's encephalopathy.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: approximately grade 2 (drowsy, disoriented to time, asterixis) — clinical estimate only, cannot be graded definitively via telehealth
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Grade 2 encephalopathy mandates hospital admission and a search for the precipitant.

risk_scores.tool_name: Maddrey / MELD (severity of alcoholic hepatitis / cirrhosis)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: cannot be calculated without bilirubin, INR and creatinine — requires hospital bloods
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Severity scoring and prognosis depend on laboratory values unobtainable by telehealth; this is itself a reason to escalate.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of tender hepatomegaly, guarding or rebound
Shifting dullness / fluid thrill confirming ascites
Serum bilirubin, LFTs, albumin, INR/coagulation, FBC, U&E, ammonia and glucose
Diagnostic ascitic tap (paracentesis) to exclude spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Abdominal ultrasound and, if indicated, CT head to exclude other causes of confusion
Blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and oxygen saturation by a validated device
Formal neurological grading of the encephalopathy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate emergency transfer to hospital by ambulance (call 000) for a confused, jaundiced, ascitic patient — for parenteral thiamine, bloods, diagnostic ascitic tap, treatment of encephalopathy and exclusion of SBP and variceal bleed. The AI Doctor recognises the pattern and escalates; it does not diagnose or treat remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising a dangerous decompensation pattern (the constellation is classic); the real risks are UNDER-action — mislabelling the confusion as intoxication, under-triaging, or failing to safety-net the variceal-bleed and SBP edges — and mis-management (sedation, or glucose before thiamine).

guidelines_referenced.reference: EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Management of Alcohol-related Liver Disease (2018)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute decompensation of alcohol-related cirrhosis with encephalopathy requires hospital admission, identification and treatment of the precipitant, and exclusion of infection (including SBP) and bleeding.

guidelines_referenced.reference: AASLD/EASL Practice Guidance on Hepatic Encephalopathy (2014)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Overt hepatic encephalopathy warrants a search for precipitating factors (infection, GI bleed, constipation, electrolyte disturbance) and inpatient management; avoid sedatives.
